---
title: "ISA 401: Business Intelligence & Data Visualization"
subtitle: '06: Web Scraping II in `r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("r-project"), fill = "white")`'
author: '<br>Fadel M. Megahed, PhD <br><br>Raymond E. Glos Professor in Business <br> Farmer School of Business<br> Miami University<br><br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("linkedin"), fill = "white")` @Fadel Megahed](https://www.linkedin.com/in/fadel-megahed-289046b4) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("github"), fill = "white")` fmegahed](https://github.com/fmegahed/) <br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("paper-plane", style = "solid"), fill = "white")` fmegahed@miamioh.edu](mailto:fmegahed@miamioh.edu)<br> [`r icons::icon_style(icons::fontawesome("question"), fill = "white")` Automated Scheduler for Office Hours](https://calendly.com/fmegahed)<br><br>'
date: "Spring 2026"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    self_contained: true
    css: [default, "../../style_files/fonts.css", "../../style_files/my-theme.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      highlightStyle: magula
      highlightLines: true
      highlightLanguage: ["r"]
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: "16:9"
header-includes:
  - "../../style_files/header.html"
---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(cache = FALSE,
                      echo = TRUE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      progress = FALSE,
                      verbose = FALSE,
                      dev = 'png',
                      fig.height = 2.5,
                      dpi = 300,
                      fig.align = 'center')

options(htmltools.dir.version = FALSE)

miamired = '#C3142D'

if(require(pacman)==FALSE) install.packages("pacman")
if(require(devtools)==FALSE) install.packages("devtools")

if(require(countdown)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/countdown")
if(require(xaringanExtra)==FALSE) devtools::install_github("gadenbuie/xaringanExtra")


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, magrittr, lubridate, janitor, # data analysis pkgs
               rvest, polite, purrr, # for web scraping
               fontawesome, RefManageR, xaringanExtra, countdown) # for slides
```

```{r xaringan-themer, include=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
if(require(xaringanthemer) == FALSE) install.packages("xaringanthemer")
library(xaringanthemer)

xaringanthemer::style_mono_accent(base_color = "#84d6d3",
                  base_font_size = "20px")

xaringanExtra::use_xaringan_extra(c("tile_view", "animate_css", "tachyons", "panelset", "search", "fit_screen", "editable",
                                    "clipable"))
```


## Quick Refresher from Web Scraping I

`r emo::ji("check")` Understand the structure of HTML and how `robots.txt` governs scraping permissions.

`r emo::ji("check")` Parse HTML documents using `rvest::read_html()`.

`r emo::ji("check")` Extract text and attributes from HTML elements with CSS selectors.

`r emo::ji("check")` Handle missing data and inconsistencies when scraping.

---

## Kahoot/Quiz Review: HTML & CSS Selectors

In class, we will go over the Kahoot titled: "06: Web Scraping, Git, Docker and HF". The Kahoot is comprised of 9 questions.

```{bash eval=FALSE, include = F}
# install Docker Desktop and open it

# In CMD open app repo and type the following:
docker build -t querychat .

# -p maps the port on your local machine to the port inside the container
docker run -p 7860:7860 querychat

# open it in http://localhost:7860/
```


---

## Learning Objectives for Today's Class

- **LO1:** Build reusable scraping functions

- **LO2:** Iterate over multiple pages using `purrr::map()`

- **LO3:** Implement polite scraping with delays

- **LO4:** Handle errors gracefully with `possibly()`


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Building Scraping Functions
### LO1: Reusable scraping functions

---

## Why Functions? The DRY Principle

.pull-left[
**D**on't **R**epeat **Y**ourself

- Copy-pasting scraping code for every URL is **error-prone** and **tedious**

- Functions let you:
  * **Encapsulate** scraping logic in one place
  * **Parameterize** inputs (e.g., the URL)
  * **Test** once, **reuse** everywhere
  * **Debug** in a single location

- A scraping function typically takes a **URL** as input and returns a **tibble** (or a named list) as output
]

.pull-right[
```r
# BAD: copy-paste approach
page1 <- rvest::read_html("https://example.com/page/1")
title1 <- page1 |> rvest::html_elements("h2") |> rvest::html_text2()

page2 <- rvest::read_html("https://example.com/page/2")
title2 <- page2 |> rvest::html_elements("h2") |> rvest::html_text2()

# Repeat for every page...
```

```r
# GOOD: function approach
scrape_page <- function(url) {
  page <- rvest::read_html(url)
  titles <- page |> rvest::html_elements("h2") |> rvest::html_text2()
  tibble::tibble(title = titles)
}

# Reuse for any URL
scrape_page("https://example.com/page/1")
scrape_page("https://example.com/page/2")
```
]

---

## From Script to Function: Step by Step

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Step 1: Working Script]

Start with code that works for **one page**:

```{r script-step1, eval=FALSE}
# Scrape a single Wikipedia page about a plane crash
url <- "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aviation_accidents_and_incidents"

page <- rvest::read_html(url)

# Extract all external links from the page
links <- page |>
  rvest::html_elements("a") |>
  rvest::html_attr("href")

text <- page |>
  rvest::html_elements("a") |>
  rvest::html_text2()

result <- tibble::tibble(link_text = text, url = links)
head(result)
```
]

.panel[.panel-name[Step 2: Identify Inputs]

Ask yourself: **What changes between runs?**

- The **URL** changes -- everything else stays the same
- The **CSS selectors** might also vary across sites

```r
# These are your function PARAMETERS:
url <- "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aviation_accidents_and_incidents"
#       ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
#       This changes!
```
]

.panel[.panel-name[Step 3: Wrap in function()]

.font90[
```{r script-step3, eval=FALSE}
scrape_links <- function(url) {

  page <- rvest::read_html(url)

  links <- page |>
    rvest::html_elements("a") |>
    rvest::html_attr("href")

  text <- page |>
    rvest::html_elements("a") |>
    rvest::html_text2()

  tibble::tibble(
    link_text = text,
    link_url = links,
    source_page = url
  )
}

# Now use it
scrape_links("https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aviation_accidents_and_incidents")
```
]
]
]


---

## Anatomy of a Good Scraping Function

```{r anatomy-func, eval=FALSE}
scrape_job_details <- function(url) {

  # 1. Read the page (may be preceded by the httr2 approach to handle cookies, headers, etc.)
  page <- rvest::read_html(url)

  # 2. Extract each field with targeted selectors
  title <- page |> rvest::html_element("h1.job-title") |> rvest::html_text2()
  company <- page |> rvest::html_element("span.company-name") |> rvest::html_text2()
  salary <- page |> rvest::html_element("div.salary") |> rvest::html_text2()
  location <- page |> rvest::html_element("span.location") |> rvest::html_text2()

  # 3. Return a one-row tibble
  tibble::tibble(
    job_title = title,
    company = company,
    salary = salary,
    location = location,
    source_url = url
  )
}
```

**Key principles:** one URL in `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right", fill = miamired)` one tibble row out, descriptive column names, include the source URL for traceability.

---

## Live Demo: Scraping Wikipedia Tables

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[The Goal]

Scrape the table of aviation accidents from a specific *PlaneCrashInfo* year page and return it as a clean tibble.

**Target:** [List of accidents and incidents involving commercial aircraft (2026)](https://www.planecrashinfo.com/2026/2026.htm)

]

.panel[.panel-name[Building the Function]

```{r scrape_fn, eval=FALSE}
scrape_plane_crashes <- function(year) {

  url <- paste0(
    "https://www.planecrashinfo.com/",
    year, "/", year, ".htm"
  )

  req <- rvest::read_html_live(url)

  # Wikipedia tables can be extracted directly
  tables <- page |> rvest::html_elements("table") |> rvest::html_table(header = T)

  # Return data frame
  return(tables[[1]])
}
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Testing It]

```{r wiki-test, eval=FALSE}
# Test with a single year
accidents_2026 <- scrape_plane_crashes(2026)
dplyr::glimpse(accidents_2026)
```

Always test your function on a single case before scaling up!

]

]

---

## `r fontawesome::fa("laptop-code", fill = miamired)` Activity: Write Your Own Scraping Function

Write a function called `scrape_quotes()` that:

1. Takes a **page number** as input
2. Constructs the URL: `http://quotes.toscrape.com/page/{number}/`
3. Extracts the **quote text**, **author**, and **tags**
4. Returns a tibble with columns: `quote`, `author`, `tags`, `page`

.pull-left[
**Starter code:**
.font80[
```r
scrape_quotes <- function(page_num) {
  url <- paste0(
    "https://quotes.toscrape.com/page/",
    page_num, "/"
  )
  page <- rvest::read_html(url)
  # YOUR CODE HERE:
  # Extract quotes, authors, tags | Return a tibble
}
```
]
]

.pull-right[
**Hints:**
- Quotes are in `.text` elements
- Authors are in `.author` elements
- Tags are in `.tag` elements (multiple per quote)

```{r activity-timer1, echo=FALSE}
countdown::countdown(minutes = 5, top = 0, right = 0,
          font_size = "2em",
          color_running_background = miamired)
```
]


---

## LO1 Check-in `r emo::ji("check")`

You should now be able to:

- Explain **why** wrapping scraping code in a function is preferable to copy-paste

- Identify which parts of a scraping script should become **function parameters**

- Write a function that takes a URL (or page parameter), scrapes data, and returns a **tibble**


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Scaling Up with purrr
### LO2: Iterating over multiple pages

---

## Why purrr::map()?

.pull-left[
.font90[
**The problem:** You have a function that scrapes one page, but you need to scrape **many** pages.

**Option 1: for loop**
```r
results <- list()
for (i in 1:10) {
  results[[i]] <- scrape_page(urls[i])
}
dplyr::bind_rows(results)
```

**Option 2: purrr::map() `r emo::ji("check")`**
```r
purrr::map(urls, scrape_page) |> dplyr::bind_rows()
```

`purrr::map()` is **cleaner**, **more readable**, and integrates naturally with the tidyverse.
]
]

.pull-right[
```{r map-diagram, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="map() applies a function to each element of a list/vector and returns a list of results.", out.width="95%"}
knitr::include_graphics("https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/f0494d020aa517ae7b1011cea4c4a9f21702df8b/2577b/diagrams/functionals/map.png")
```

.footnote[
<html><hr></html>
**Source:** [Hadley Wickham, Advanced R (2nd ed.), Ch. 9](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/functionals.html)
]
]

---

## The map() Family at a Glance

| Function | Returns | Use case |
|----------|---------|----------|
| `map()` | A **list** | When outputs vary in structure |
| `map_chr()` | A **character** vector | Extracting single strings |
| `map_dbl()` | A **double** vector | Extracting single numbers |
| `map_lgl()` | A **logical** vector | TRUE/FALSE checks |
| `map_dfr()` | A **data frame** (row-bound) | Combining tibbles from each iteration |
| `map_df()` | Same as `map_dfr()` | Shorthand alias |

For scraping, **`map_dfr()`** is your best friend -- it applies the function to each input and row-binds the resulting tibbles into one data frame.

```r
all_quotes <- purrr::map_dfr(1:10, scrape_quotes)
```

---

## Demo: Scraping Multiple Years of Accidents

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Setup]

We already built `scrape_plane_crashes()` for one year. Now let's scale it to **6 years** (2021--2026).

```{r multi-year-setup, eval=FALSE}
years <- 2021:2026
```

**Goal:** One clean data frame with all accident records across these years.

]

.panel[.panel-name[Using map_dfr]

```{r multi-year-map, eval=FALSE}
all_accidents <- purrr::map_dfr(years, scrape_plane_crashes)

# How many records total?
nrow(all_accidents)

# Records per year
all_accidents |> dplyr::count(year)
```

That is it -- one line of code replaces a loop that would span 10+ lines.

]

.panel[.panel-name[Adding Delays]

**Important:** When scraping multiple pages, add a delay between requests to avoid overwhelming the server.

```{r multi-year-delay, eval=FALSE}
scrape_plane_crashes_polite <- function(year) {
  Sys.sleep(1)  # Wait 1 second between requests
  scrape_plane_crashes(year)
}

all_accidents <- purrr::map_dfr(years, scrape_plane_crashes_polite)
dplyr::glimpse(all_accidents)
```

]

.panel[.panel-name[Quick Analysis]

```{r multi-year-analysis, eval=FALSE}
# Accidents by year
all_accidents |>
  dplyr::count(year) |>
  ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = year, y = n)) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(fill = miamired) +
  ggplot2::labs(title = "Commercial Aviation Accidents by Year",
       x = "Year", y = "Number of Incidents") +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal()
```

]

]

---

## Job Market Connection `r fontawesome::fa("briefcase", fill = miamired)`

.pull-left[
**Building a scraping pipeline = automating your job search research**

Imagine you want to track Data Analyst job postings across multiple company career pages:

```r
# Define company career page URLs
google_url <- "https://www.google.com/about/careers/applications/jobs/results/?q=data%20analyst&page=" # where page number goes from 1 to 9

# List of URLs to scrape
career_urls <- paste0(
  "https://www.google.com/about/careers/applications/jobs/results/?q=data%20analyst&page=",
  1:9, 
)

# Write your function to scrape job titles, companies, locations, etc. from each page

# Scrape all with one line
all_jobs <- purrr::map_dfr(career_urls, scrape_careers)
```
]

.pull-right[
**This skill is directly transferable to:**

- Market research (scraping competitor pricing across pages)

- Academic research (collecting data from multiple sources)

- Business dashboards (automated data collection pipelines)

- Any scenario where the **same structure** repeats across **many URLs**
]

---

## LO2 Check-in `r emo::ji("check")`

You should now be able to:

- Explain when and why to use `purrr::map()` instead of a for loop

- Use `map_dfr()` to apply a scraping function over multiple inputs and combine results

- Add `Sys.sleep()` between requests to pace your scraping


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Being a Polite Scraper
### LO3: Rate limiting and the polite package

---

## Why Rate Limiting Matters

.pull-left[
**Without rate limiting, you risk:**

- `r fontawesome::fa("ban", fill = miamired)` Getting your **IP blocked** by the server

- `r fontawesome::fa("server", fill = miamired)` **Overloading** small websites (especially non-commercial ones)

- `r fontawesome::fa("gavel", fill = miamired)` Violating the site's **Terms of Service**

- `r fontawesome::fa("exclamation-triangle", fill = miamired)` Receiving **incomplete** or **error** responses (HTTP 429: Too Many Requests)
]

.pull-right[
**Best practices:**

1. Always check `robots.txt` first

2. Add delays between requests (1--5 seconds is common)

3. Identify yourself with a meaningful `User-Agent` string

4. Scrape during **off-peak hours** when possible

5. **Cache** results so you do not re-scrape needlessly

6. Only scrape what you **need**
]

---

## The Simple Approach: Sys.sleep()

The quickest way to add delays is `Sys.sleep()`:

```{r sys-sleep-demo, eval=FALSE}
scrape_with_delay <- function(url, delay = 1) {
  Sys.sleep(delay)  # Pause before each request

  page <- rvest::read_html(url)

  titles <- page |>
    rvest::html_elements("h2") |>
    rvest::html_text2()

  tibble::tibble(title = titles, source = url)
}

# Use with map
urls <- paste0("http://quotes.toscrape.com/page/", 1:5, "/")
results <- purrr::map_dfr(urls, ~scrape_with_delay(.x, delay = 2))
```

**Pros:** Simple, no extra packages.
**Cons:** Does not check `robots.txt` automatically, no session management, no built-in caching.

---

## The polite Package: bow() + scrape()

.pull-left[
.font90[
The `polite` package automates respectful scraping:

1. **`bow(url)`** -- Introduce yourself to the server
   - Checks `robots.txt`
   - Sets a polite `User-Agent`
   - Stores session info

2. **`scrape(session)`** -- Fetch the page
   - Respects crawl delay from `robots.txt`
   - Automatically throttles requests

3. **`nod(session, path)`** -- Navigate to a new path within the same domain
]
]

.pull-right[
.font90[
```{r polite-basic, eval=FALSE}
# Step 1: Bow (introduce yourself)
session <- polite::bow(
  url = "https://quotes.toscrape.com",
  user_agent = "ISA401 Student Bot (educational)"
)

# See what robots.txt says
print(session)

# Step 2: Scrape the page
page <- polite::scrape(session)

# Step 3: Extract data as usual with rvest
quotes <- page |>
  rvest::html_elements(".text") |>
  rvest::html_text2()

head(quotes)
```
]
]

---

## polite in a Multi-Page Pipeline

```{r polite-pipeline, eval=FALSE}
scrape_quotes_polite <- function(page_num) {

  # Establish session with bow()
  session <- polite::bow(
    url = "http://quotes.toscrape.com",
    user_agent = "ISA401 Student Bot (educational)"
  )

  # Navigate to the specific page with nod()
  session <- polite::nod(session, path = paste0("page/", page_num, "/"))

  # Scrape the page (respects robots.txt and crawl delay)
  page <- polite::scrape(session)

  # Extract data
  quotes <- page |> rvest::html_elements(".text") |> rvest::html_text2()
  authors <- page |> rvest::html_elements(".author") |> rvest::html_text2()

  tibble::tibble(quote = quotes, author = authors, page = page_num)
}

# Scrape 5 pages politely
all_quotes <- purrr::map_dfr(1:5, scrape_quotes_polite)
nrow(all_quotes)
```


---

## LO3 Check-in `r emo::ji("check")`

You should now be able to:

- Explain **why** rate limiting and polite scraping matter

- Use `Sys.sleep()` to introduce delays between requests

- Use the `polite` package workflow: `bow()` `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right", fill = miamired)` `nod()` `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right", fill = miamired)` `scrape()`


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Graceful Error Handling
### LO4: Using possibly() and tryCatch()

---

## The Problem: One Bad URL Breaks Everything

.pull-left[
Imagine you are scraping 50 pages and **page 23** has been taken down:
```{r error-problem, eval=FALSE}
urls <- paste0(
  "http://quotes.toscrape.com/page/", 1:50, "/"
)

# This will STOP at the first error
results <- purrr::map_dfr(urls, scrape_quotes) #<<
```

`r fontawesome::fa("exclamation-circle", fill = miamired)` **Result:** You lose results from pages 1--22 and never reach 24--50!
]

.pull-right[
**Common causes of mid-pipeline errors:**

- A page has been **removed** (HTTP 404)

- The server is **temporarily down** (HTTP 503)

- The page **structure changed** (missing CSS class)

- A **timeout** occurred on one request

- The page **requires authentication**

**We need a way to skip errors and keep going.**
]

---

## tryCatch(): The Base R Approach

`tryCatch()` lets you **catch** errors and return a fallback value instead of crashing:

```{r trycatch-demo, eval=FALSE}
scrape_safe <- function(url) {
  tryCatch(
    {
      # The code that might fail
      page <- rvest::read_html(url)
      titles <- page |> rvest::html_elements("h2") |> rvest::html_text2()
      tibble::tibble(title = titles, url = url)
    },
    error = function(e) {
      # What to do if it fails
      message("Failed to scrape: ", url, " - ", e$message)
      tibble::tibble(title = NA_character_, url = url)
    }
  )
}

# Now this won't crash on bad URLs
scrape_safe("https://httpstat.us/404")  # Returns tibble with NA
scrape_safe("http://quotes.toscrape.com/page/1/")  # Returns real data
```

**Pros:** Full control over error behavior. **Cons:** Verbose, repetitive.

---

## purrr::possibly(): The Elegant Wrapper

`possibly()` creates a **modified version** of your function that returns a default value on error instead of stopping:

```{r possibly-intro, eval=FALSE}
# Original function (will crash on bad URLs)
scrape_quotes  # our function from earlier

# Wrapped function (returns NULL on error)
safe_scrape_quotes <- purrr::possibly(scrape_quotes, otherwise = NULL) #<<
```

.pull-left[
**How it works:**
.font90[
- `possibly(f, otherwise)` returns a **new function**
- The new function behaves exactly like `f`...
- ...except when `f` throws an error, it returns `otherwise` instead
]
]

.pull-right[
```r
# These are equivalent:
safe_scrape_quotes(1)  # works normally
safe_scrape_quotes(999)  # returns NULL
                         # (no page 999 exists)
```
]

---

## possibly() in a Full Pipeline

```{r possibly-pipeline, eval=FALSE}
# Step 1: Define your scraping function
scrape_quotes <- function(page_num) {
  url <- paste0("http://quotes.toscrape.com/page/", page_num, "/")
  Sys.sleep(1)
  page <- rvest::read_html(url)
  quotes <- page |> rvest::html_elements(".text") |> rvest::html_text2()
  authors <- page |> rvest::html_elements(".author") |> rvest::html_text2()
  tibble::tibble(quote = quotes, author = authors, page = page_num)
}

# Step 2: Wrap with possibly()
safe_scrape <- purrr::possibly(scrape_quotes, otherwise = NULL) #<<

# Step 3: Map over inputs (including some that might fail)
page_numbers <- c(1:5, 999, 6:10)  # page 999 doesn't exist

results <- purrr::map(page_numbers, safe_scrape) |> #<<
  purrr::compact() |>  # Remove NULLs (failed pages) #<<
  dplyr::bind_rows()   # Combine successful results #<<

nrow(results)  # All successful pages combined
```

**Key insight:** `compact()` removes `NULL` entries from a list, so only successful scrapes are kept.

---

## Side-by-Side: With vs. Without Error Handling

.panelset[

.panel[.panel-name[Without Error Handling]

```{r no-error-handling, eval=FALSE}
# This will CRASH when it hits a bad page
scrape_quotes <- function(page_num) {
  url <- paste0("http://quotes.toscrape.com/page/",
                page_num, "/")
  page <- rvest::read_html(url)
  quotes <- page |> rvest::html_elements(".text") |> rvest::html_text2()
  authors <- page |> rvest::html_elements(".author") |> rvest::html_text2()
  tibble::tibble(quote = quotes, author = authors, page = page_num)
}

# Risky: one error kills everything
pages <- c(1, 2, 999, 3, 4)
results <- purrr::map_dfr(pages, scrape_quotes)
# Error in open.connection(x, "rb") :
#   HTTP error 404. ---- PIPELINE STOPS HERE
```

`r fontawesome::fa("times-circle", fill = miamired)` Pages 3 and 4 are never reached.

]

.panel[.panel-name[With possibly()]

```{r with-error-handling, eval=FALSE}
# Same function, but wrapped safely
safe_scrape <- purrr::possibly(scrape_quotes, otherwise = NULL)

# Resilient: errors are caught silently
pages <- c(1, 2, 999, 3, 4)
results <- purrr::map(pages, safe_scrape) |>
  purrr::compact() |>
  dplyr::bind_rows()

nrow(results)  # Contains data from pages 1, 2, 3, 4
# Page 999 was skipped gracefully
```

`r emo::ji("check")` All valid pages are successfully scraped.

]

.panel[.panel-name[With Logging]

```{r with-logging, eval=FALSE}
# Track which pages succeeded/failed
safe_scrape_verbose <- function(page_num) {
  result <- tryCatch(
    scrape_quotes(page_num),
    error = function(e) {
      message("FAILED page ", page_num, ": ", e$message)
      NULL
    }
  )
  if (!is.null(result)) message("OK page ", page_num)
  result
}

pages <- c(1, 2, 999, 3, 4)
results <- purrr::map(pages, safe_scrape_verbose) |>
  purrr::compact() |>
  dplyr::bind_rows()
# Messages:
# OK page 1
# OK page 2
# FAILED page 999: HTTP error 404.
# OK page 3
# OK page 4
```

]

]


---

## `r fontawesome::fa("laptop-code", fill = miamired)` Activity: Write, Test, and Wrap with possibly()

**Task:** Build a scraping function, test it, and make it error-resilient.

.pull-left[
**Step 1:** Write `scrape_book()` to scrape a single book page from [books.toscrape.com](http://books.toscrape.com):
```r
scrape_book <- function(page_num) {
  url <- paste0(
    "http://books.toscrape.com/catalogue/page-",
    page_num, ".html"
  )
  page <- rvest::read_html(url)
  titles <- page |>
    rvest::html_elements("h3 a") |> rvest::html_attr("title")
  prices <- page |>
    rvest::html_elements(".price_color") |> rvest::html_text2()
  tibble::tibble(title = titles, price = prices,
         page = page_num)
}
```
]

.pull-right[
**Step 2:** Test on valid and invalid pages:
```r
scrape_book(1)   # Should work
scrape_book(999) # Should error
```

**Step 3:** Wrap with `possibly()` and scrape pages 1--5 plus page 999:
```r
safe_scrape_book <- purrr::possibly(
  scrape_book, otherwise = NULL
)

results <- purrr::map(c(1:5, 999), safe_scrape_book) |>
  purrr::compact() |>
  dplyr::bind_rows()

nrow(results)  # Should be 100 (20 per page x 5)
```

```{r activity-timer2, echo=FALSE}
countdown::countdown(minutes = 6, top = 0, right = 0,
          font_size = "2em",
          color_running_background = miamired)
```
]


---

## LO4 Check-in `r emo::ji("check")`

You should now be able to:

- Explain why **error handling** is essential in multi-page scraping pipelines

- Use `tryCatch()` to catch errors and return fallback values

- Use `purrr::possibly()` to wrap functions for safe iteration

- Combine `possibly()`, `map()`, `compact()`, and `bind_rows()` into a resilient pipeline


---
class: inverse, center, middle

# Recap

---

## Summary of Main Points

By now, you should be able to do the following:

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO1:** Build reusable scraping functions -- encapsulate scraping logic, parameterize URLs, return tidy tibbles

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO2:** Iterate over multiple pages with `purrr::map_dfr()` -- scale from one page to hundreds with a single line

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO3:** Scrape politely -- use `Sys.sleep()` and the `polite` package (`bow()` / `nod()` / `scrape()`) to respect servers

`r emo::ji("check")` **LO4:** Handle errors gracefully -- use `possibly()` so one failed request does not crash your entire pipeline


---

## Readings and Resources

.pull-left[
**Essential:**

- [rvest documentation](https://rvest.tidyverse.org/)

- [purrr::map() reference](https://purrr.tidyverse.org/reference/map.html)

- [polite package vignette](https://dmi3kno.github.io/polite/)

- [R for Data Science (2e): Ch. 24 -- Web Scraping](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/webscraping)
]

.pull-right[
**Supplementary:**

- [Advanced R: Functionals (Ch. 9)](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/functionals.html) -- deep dive into `map()` and friends

- [purrr tutorial by Jenny Bryan](https://jennybc.github.io/purrr-tutorial/)

- [SelectorGadget Chrome Extension](https://chrome.google.com/webstore/detail/selectorgadget/mhjhnkcfbdhnjickkkdbjoemdmbfginb) -- interactive CSS selector tool
]


---

## Next Steps

- **Homework:** Practice building a scraping pipeline that scrapes multiple pages and handles errors

- **Coming Up Next:** LLM-based text extraction -- using large language models to parse unstructured text from web pages and documents

- **Key Takeaway:** The pattern `write function` `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right", fill = miamired)` `wrap with possibly()` `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right", fill = miamired)` `map over inputs` `r fontawesome::fa("arrow-right", fill = miamired)` `combine results` is fundamental to scalable data collection in `r fontawesome::fa("r-project", fill = miamired)`
